pat.title: Personal API tokens
pat.name: Name
pat.secretOnce: The full value is shown once and will never be available again.

No clusters available. Check backend con: No clusters available. Check backend configuration or wait for service discovery.

chart.clickToCopy: Click to copy the Query ID

report.cpuTimeUnavailable: CPU time could not be computed. The query is likely running in parallel — pg_stat_statements sums I/O time across all workers, but execution time only for the leader process.

compare.storageUnavailable: Snapshot storage is not configured. Comparison is unavailable.
compare.stats: Total: {total} | In both: {both} | Only in A: {onlyA} | Only in B: {onlyB}

autosnapshot.resetQueryStatsHint: "Reset pgss after a snapshot" — pg_stat_statements is reset after every auto snapshot, so the next snapshot holds a clean delta; the reset function can be overridden via pgss_reset_function in dasha.yaml if the role lacks permission for pg_stat_statements_reset().

hot.title: Hot objects
hot.hint: Delta snapshots of table/index activity: an exact top per metric class plus a tail histogram. Collected from every cluster host on a schedule.
hot.fieldsHint: Schedule — 5-field cron, UTC time (own zone: CRON_TZ=Europe/Moscow 0 3 * * *; examples: 0 3 * * * — daily at 03:00, */30 * * * * — every half hour). Top size — how many objects are stored by name per metric class; the rest folds into the tail histogram.
hot.scheduleInvalid: Not a cron expression: 5 fields expected, e.g. 0 3 * * * or */30 * * * *

locks.title: Locks
locks.hint: Capture the lock graph when the spike trigger fires

autosnapshot.spikeFieldsHint: "Snapshot on recovery" — after a spike, take a snapshot once activity has stayed below the threshold for the given time (captures the aftermath). "Deferred snapshot" — after a spike snapshot, queue one more after the given interval (the queue survives restarts). 0s in either field — disabled.
autosnapshot.saveInvalid: Settings not saved: the server rejected the values — check the cron expression and numeric fields.

clusters.name: Cluster
clusters.loadError: Failed to load the cluster settings

hint.cacheHitRates: Computed from pg_statio — cumulative statistics since the last reset
hint.tableBloat: Bloat is estimated from pg_stats (null_frac, avg_width). Accuracy depends on how recently ANALYZE ran
hint.indexBloat: Bloat is estimated from pg_class and pg_stats — use pgstattuple for exact figures
hint.missingIndexes: Estimated rows — a rough figure from pg_class.reltuples
hint.indexUsage: Index usage percentage — estimated from cumulative scan statistics
hint.tableCaching: Hit rate is computed from pg_statio — cumulative counters since statistics were last reset
hint.indexCaching: Hit rate is computed from pg_statio — cumulative counters since statistics were last reset
hint.queryStats: Query statistics from pg_stat_statements — cumulative since the last reset. Percentages are shares over that whole period
hint.maintenanceInfo: n_dead_tup / n_live_tup are estimates, refreshed by VACUUM/ANALYZE
hint.partitions: Partition sizes are estimated from pg_class.relpages

tables.statHintLayers: storage layers: main data, long values (TOAST), free space map, visibility map
tables.statHintDml: percentage of inserted / updated / deleted rows
tables.statHintHotUpd: row updates using the HOT update optimization (% of all updates)
tables.statHintSeqScn: percentage of sequential / index scans (of the total)

queries.queryFilterPlaceholder: % and _ are wildcards; empty = no filter

indexes.btreeOnArrayHint: Non-unique BTree indexes on array columns. BTree cannot search for element containment in an array — a GIN index may be the right choice.
indexes.similar1Hint: Non-unique indexes that are a subset of a unique index on the same table.
indexes.similar2Hint: Pairs of foreign keys that reference the same columns and may be redundant.
indexes.similar3Hint: Indexes with similar definitions on the same table.
indexes.allHostsCheck: Analyse every host in the cluster (may be slow)

progress.numDeadTuplesHint: Counter of the current VACUUM cycle: dead-row pointers collected since the last index pass (resets after each pass). This is not the table's total dead rows — that lives on the Maintenance page (n_dead_tup). Before PG 17 the second number is the buffer capacity derived from maintenance_work_mem.
progress.deadTupleBytesHint: PG 17+: used / limit of the TID-store memory (autovacuum_work_mem or maintenance_work_mem). When the limit is reached, VACUUM pauses the heap scan for an index vacuum pass.

index.building index: loading tuples in tree: Building index: loading tuples in tree
index.waiting for readers before marking dead: Waiting for readers before marking dead

baseBackup.waiting for wal archiving to finish: Waiting for WAL archiving to finish

home.statsNeverReset: Cumulative statistics have never been reset
home.pgssStatsNeverReset: pg_stat_statements statistics have never been reset

hint.deadlocks: pg_stat_database.deadlocks — the number of deadlocks detected. A deadlock means PostgreSQL had to abort one of the transactions
hint.checksumFailures: pg_stat_database.checksum_failures — page checksum errors. A value > 0 signals data corruption on disk
hint.conflicts: pg_stat_database.conflicts — queries cancelled because of conflicts with WAL recovery on a replica (recovery conflicts)
hint.rollbackRatio: Rollback share: xact_rollback / (xact_commit + xact_rollback). A high percentage may point to application errors or frequent deadlocks
hint.statsSince: pg_stat_database.stats_reset — every counter (deadlocks, conflicts, rollbacks) has accumulated since this moment

home.lowHitRateWarning: Hit rate is below {threshold}% — consider increasing shared_buffers

logs.noResults: No logs found for the selected period
logs.selectCluster: Select a cluster to view its logs
logs.notSupported: Log search is not available for this cluster
logs.partial: Not all records are shown: the scan limit was reached ({scanned}). Narrow the filters or shorten the period.

chart.title: Log frequency by severity
chart.zoomHint: select a range, or click a bar, to set the period in the filters

error.rateLimited: Too many log requests — try again in {seconds} s
error.upstream: Error calling the Yandex Cloud API
error.timeout: Timed out waiting for a response from Yandex Cloud
error.unsupported: Log search is not available for this cluster
error.network: The server did not respond (network error or dropped connection). Yandex Cloud may be taking too long — retry the request or shorten the period.

stateHint.streaming: Streaming — the replica is receiving the WAL stream in real time
stateHint.catchup: Catchup — the replica is catching up with the primary and has not reached the current position yet
stateHint.startup: Startup — the replica is starting up and establishing a connection
stateHint.backup: Backup — a pg_basebackup is running from this server
stateHint.stopping: Stopping — the WAL sender is shutting down and the replica is disconnecting

syncHint.async: Async — the primary does not wait for an acknowledgement from this replica
syncHint.sync: Sync — the primary waits for this replica to acknowledge the WAL write before committing
syncHint.quorum: Quorum — the replica takes part in quorum acknowledgement (synchronous_standby_names)
syncHint.potential: Potential — may become synchronous if the current synchronous replica fails

replication.syncStandbyHint: Defines which replicas take part in synchronous replication. The primary waits for the listed replicas to acknowledge the WAL write before confirming the commit to the client. An empty value means fully asynchronous replication.

syncCommitHint.on: The commit completes once the synchronous replicas confirm the WAL has reached durable storage (fsync). Guarantees durability if any single server fails
syncCommitHint.remote_apply: The commit completes once the transaction has been applied on the replica — the data is immediately visible to queries. Highest latency, but readability is guaranteed
syncCommitHint.remote_write: The commit completes once the WAL has been written to the replica's filesystem (without fsync). Protects against a PostgreSQL crash on the replica, but not against an OS crash
syncCommitHint.local: The commit waits only for the local fsync; replicas take no part in the acknowledgement. Synchronous replication is effectively disabled
syncCommitHint.off: No wait, not even for the local fsync. Fastest, but the most recent transactions may be lost on a crash (database integrity is still preserved)

walStatusHint.reserved: The slot's WAL files fit within max_wal_size
walStatusHint.extended: max_wal_size is exceeded, but the files are retained (wal_keep_size, or the slot needs them)
walStatusHint.unreserved: WAL files are no longer reserved; some will be removed at the next checkpoint
walStatusHint.lost: The required WAL files are lost and the slot is no longer usable

fk.invalidConstraintsHint: Constraints marked NOT VALID that still need validation.
fk.typeMismatchHint: Foreign keys whose column types differ between the source and the referenced table.
fk.possibleNullsHint: Foreign keys whose columns allow NULL — they may need a NOT NULL constraint.
fk.possibleSimilarHint: Pairs of foreign keys on the same table that may be redundant.

settings.name: Parameter
settings.noIssues: No problems found in the settings

n.logMinDurationTooLow: log_min_duration_statement is set too low ({value}) — queries are logged excessively
n.collapseLimitDiffer: {name} differs from the default ({value}) — planning time may increase
n.hugePagesDisabled: huge_pages is disabled ({value}) — enabling it is recommended to reduce memory-management overhead
n.autovacuumDisabled: autovacuum is disabled — this is dangerous for database stability
n.tuneWorkMem: autovacuum_work_mem is too small (effective value: {value} kB, ≤ 64 MB)
n.tooManyCheckpoints: Too many forced checkpoints (req: {req}, timed: {timed}) — increase max_wal_size

describe.selectTable: Select a table — follow a link from the “Tables overview” section
describe.bloatCalcHint: Estimates bloat via pgstattuple_approx (can be slow on large tables)
describe.bloatUnavailable: The pgstattuple extension is not installed in this database
describe.replicaVacuumHint: This host is a replica. Autovacuum and ANALYZE only run on the primary; pg_stat_user_tables on a replica reflects local operations and does not describe the real state of the table. Switch to the cluster's primary.
describe.willToast: Rows will be pushed out to TOAST

hint.lastVacuum: pg_stat_user_tables.last_vacuum — when a manual VACUUM last ran
hint.lastAutovacuum: pg_stat_user_tables.last_autovacuum — when an automatic VACUUM last ran
hint.lastAnalyze: pg_stat_user_tables.last_analyze — when a manual ANALYZE last ran
hint.lastAutoanalyze: pg_stat_user_tables.last_autoanalyze — when an automatic ANALYZE last ran
hint.deadTuples: pg_stat_user_tables.n_dead_tup — dead rows waiting to be reclaimed by VACUUM
hint.liveTuples: pg_stat_user_tables.n_live_tup — the estimated number of live rows in the table
hint.modSinceAnalyze: pg_stat_user_tables.n_mod_since_analyze — rows modified since the last ANALYZE
hint.insSinceVacuum: pg_stat_user_tables.n_ins_since_vacuum — rows inserted since the last VACUUM
hint.vacuumThreshold: Autovacuum triggers once dead tuples exceed: autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor × pg_class.reltuples
hint.analyzeThreshold: Autoanalyze triggers once modifications exceed: autovacuum_analyze_threshold + autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor × pg_class.reltuples
hint.insertVacThreshold: Insert-driven autovacuum triggers once inserts exceed: autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor × pg_class.reltuples
hint.tupleHeader: HeapTupleHeaderData — the fixed header on every row (23 bytes)
hint.nullBitmap: Bitmap of NULL values: ceil(column_count / 8) bytes
hint.rowData: The sum of avg_width from pg_stats across all the table's columns
hint.fillfactor: Percentage of a page filled with data. The remainder is reserved for HOT updates (a reloption, or 100% by default)
hint.pageUsable: block_size minus the page header (PageHeaderData = 24 bytes)
hint.availableSpace: page_usable × fillfactor / 100 — the space available for inserting new rows
hint.reservedForUpdates: page_usable × (100 − fillfactor) / 100 — the space left for HOT updates
hint.reservedForHotUpdates: How many updated versions of a row fit into the reserved space
hint.toastCandidates: Columns whose average width is ≥ the TOAST threshold (block_size / 4 ≈ 2 kB). Columns with storage = plain are never pushed out to TOAST

hot.hint: Scheduled activity-counter deltas (daily by default) collected by the autosnapshot daemon from every cluster host and summed (the counters are not replicated). An exact top per metric class is stored plus a tail histogram — coverage shows how much of the total activity landed in the top. The "Per day" column is normalized by the snapshot's actual window. Expand a row for the per-host breakdown.
hot.hostsMissing: Hosts unreachable at capture: {hosts}. Their activity is absent from every number — the snapshot is partial.

tail.summary: Outside the top: {count} objects, sum {sum} (P50 {median}, P90 {p90}, max {max})
tail.idle: Outside the top: {count} objects, all idle over the snapshot window — the top covers the entire load.
tail.hint: The distribution tail — objects that did not make the stored top. They are not kept by name, only the shape: deciles of the class key over the snapshot window. Flat bars mean a cold tail and a representative top; tall right-side bars mean many warm objects just below the top — check coverage.

hot.noSnapshots: No snapshots yet — the daemon captures on a schedule, daily by default (snapshot storage must be enabled).

describe.title: Hotness
describe.hint: Live delta since the last anchor slice: the absolute rate (class key per day) and the place in this database's activity distribution. The percentile is relative: on a quiet database "hotter than 90% of tables" does not mean real load — read the rate.
describe.allQuiet: No notable activity: ~0/day for both reads and writes, never in the hot top.
describe.topAppearances: Top appearances over the last {days} days: {n} (latest — {date})

summary.title: Autovacuum summary
summary.hint: Tables past their own autovacuum/autoanalyze trigger thresholds (PostgreSQL's formula: threshold + scale_factor × rows, per-table settings honoured; the row count is the lower of reltuples and live tuples, so stale statistics cannot hide a table), and maintenance processes currently running in this database.
summary.allOk: All tables are within thresholds — autovacuum has nothing to do right now.

healthScore.title: Health Score
healthScore.tooltip: An overall health score for the PostgreSQL instance (0–100). It accounts for connections, performance, storage, replication and maintenance.
healthScore.metricsDegraded: Metrics mode (datasource) is on and the target was matched, but no metric matched the selectors — the score is built on missing signals and may be understated. Check the label matching: GET /api/common/health-score/datasource/status.

page.title: Health Score
page.noRecommendations: All good — nothing critical to report.

about.intro: A composite metric (0–100) describing the overall state of a PostgreSQL instance across eight categories. Higher is better.
about.formulaDescription: For each category i a continuous penalty (0..100) is computed from the raw metrics and summed with weights that add up to 1.0. Weights are validated and normalised; invalid values fall back to the defaults.
about.dropIntro: Categories with no useful signal on the current instance are dropped, and their weight is redistributed proportionally across the rest:
about.dropReplication: Replication — dropped when the instance has no replicas.
about.dropMaintenance: Maintenance — dropped when pg_is_in_recovery() = true (a standby). Autovacuum and ANALYZE do not run on a replica, so the metrics describe the primary.
about.rulesEngine: In parallel, a rules engine evaluates the same metrics and produces a list of recommendations with links. Rules and penalties are independent: penalties yield the numeric score, rules yield the list of actions. Every rule contributes to the score (a penalty or a floor), so a condition never shows up in the recommendations without moving the number.
about.criticalIntro: A weighted average dilutes catastrophes. Any of the conditions below forces the final score into the red zone (≤ 30), bypassing the average — otherwise an outage would dissolve into the healthy categories. Applied on the primary only (on a standby the condition refers to the primary):
about.criticalXid: XID wraparound at failsafe: max(age(datfrozenxid), age(relfrozenxid)) ≥ 1.6 billion (vacuum_failsafe_age), where PostgreSQL itself falls back to emergency VACUUM.
about.criticalAutovacuum: autovacuum is globally disabled — bloat and XID age grow unchecked.
about.criticalTrackCounts: track_counts is disabled — autovacuum is “blind” and effectively never runs.

categoryDescriptions.connections: Connection usage, idle in transaction, long transactions
categoryDescriptions.storage: Dead tuple share, bloat, HOT update efficiency
categoryDescriptions.replication: Replication lag (time and bytes), disconnected replicas
categoryDescriptions.maintenance: XID age, vacuum staleness, the autovacuum/track_counts GUCs, ANALYZE
categoryDescriptions.horizon: MVCC horizon lag (the oldest snapshot holding VACUUM back)
categoryDescriptions.locks: Lock waiters, ungranted locks, deadlocks, lock pool saturation

about.penaltyDescription: The penalty grows continuously with the metric. The breakpoints are the metric values at which the slope of the penalty function changes: below the first point there is no penalty, between points it grows smoothly, and past the last one it reaches the category maximum. That is how the “→” arrows in the table read: first point → second point → third point.
about.xidCalibration: The XID age penalty starts at autovacuum_freeze_max_age (200 M), reaches 80 at vacuum_failsafe_age (1.6 B) and 100 at the shutdown wall of ~2.1 B; relfrozenxid follows the same curve via max(datfrozenxid, relfrozenxid).
about.rulesDescription: Each rule fires when a discrete LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH threshold is crossed. Instance-only categories are hidden when drilling down into a single database, and the whole maintenance category is hidden on a standby.

ruleNotes.high_connection_ratio: count(*) from pg_stat_activity / max_connections — the headroom before new connections are refused.
ruleNotes.idle_in_transaction: Sessions with state='idle in transaction'. Each one holds locks and pins the MVCC horizon.
ruleNotes.long_running_transaction: now() - xact_start of the longest transaction — they amplify bloat and hold back freezing.
ruleNotes.low_cache_hit_ratio: heap_blks_hit / (heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read) from pg_statio_user_tables, % — the share of reads served from shared_buffers.
ruleNotes.track_io_timing_disabled: The GUC is off — no I/O timings in pg_stat_statements or EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
ruleNotes.high_max_dead_ratio: The worst per-table n_dead_tup / (n_live_tup + n_dead_tup), % — autovacuum is not keeping up.
ruleNotes.high_avg_dead_ratio: The same ratio, averaged over tables with >1000 live rows — background bloat.
ruleNotes.many_bloated_tables: The number of tables whose dead share is above the autovacuum trigger.
ruleNotes.low_hot_update_ratio: n_tup_hot_upd / n_tup_upd — a low share means indexes grow because of UPDATEs.
ruleNotes.high_newpage_update_ratio: PG 16+: the share of UPDATEs that broke the HOT chain.
ruleNotes.replication_lag_time: EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM replay_lag) of the worst standby — how far behind WAL replay is.
ruleNotes.replication_lag_bytes: pg_current_wal_lsn() - replay_lsn — the WAL tail still to be applied.
ruleNotes.disconnected_replicas: Replicas listed in dasha.yaml that are absent from pg_stat_replication.
ruleNotes.xid_wraparound_risk: max(age(datfrozenxid)) — transactions left before wraparound. At 200M anti-wraparound autovacuum should be running; at 2B the database goes read-only.
ruleNotes.vacuum_backlog: Tables that crossed the autovacuum threshold (on dead tuples or inserts, reloptions included) — the length of the vacuum queue.
ruleNotes.stale_vacuum: Days since the last last_(auto)vacuum among overdue tables (those past their autovacuum threshold) — a sign that autovacuum is stuck.
ruleNotes.tables_never_vacuumed: Tables with last_vacuum IS NULL AND last_autovacuum IS NULL.
ruleNotes.autovacuum_disabled: Global autovacuum=off — bloat and XID age grow unchecked.
ruleNotes.track_counts_disabled: track_counts=off — autovacuum has no statistics to decide on.
ruleNotes.tables_with_autovacuum_off: Tables with autovacuum_enabled=false in their reloptions.
ruleNotes.relfrozenxid_age_outlier: The worst per-table age(relfrozenxid) — the per-table view of the XID risk.
ruleNotes.stale_planner_stats: n_mod_since_analyze is past the autoanalyze threshold with no ANALYZE for >3 hours — the planner's statistics are stale.
ruleNotes.horizon_lag_xids: txid_current() - min(backend_xmin) — how many rows VACUUM cannot remove.
ruleNotes.requested_checkpoint_ratio: checkpoints_req / total — a high share means max_wal_size is small, or there is a write spike.
ruleNotes.wal_level_minimal_with_replicas: wal_level=minimal cannot do physical replication — the replicas are silently broken.
ruleNotes.wal_level_logical_without_publications: wal_level=logical, but pg_publication is empty — extra WAL for nothing.
ruleNotes.active_lock_waiters: Sessions with wait_event_type='Lock' — blocked right now.
ruleNotes.longest_lock_wait_seconds: EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM now() - state_change) of the longest lock wait.
ruleNotes.ungranted_locks: Rows in pg_locks with granted=false — the queue behind the holder.
ruleNotes.deadlocks_rate: The cumulative deadlocks counter since pg_stat_database_reset — without a per-day rate, the useful signal is simply “>0”.
ruleNotes.checksum_failures: Any non-zero rate of checksum errors means data corruption; it also forces the score into the red zone.
ruleNotes.host_cpu_saturation: load_avg_15 / n_vcpu. Above 1 the CPU queue exceeds the core count.
ruleNotes.pooler_saturation: server_conns / pool_size of the pooler; approaching 1.0 puts clients in a queue.
ruleNotes.sequence_exhaustion: The worst sequence usage share; at ≥0.95 it forces the score down (writes stop on overflow).
ruleNotes.latency_regression: Current latency / seasonal baseline (hour × weekday); works on any workload.
ruleNotes.seq_scan_regression: Current seq-scan row rate / seasonal baseline; growth means indexes are unused or statistics went stale (ANALYZE).
ruleNotes.host_disk_space: The used share of the most-utilised filesystem on the host (pgSCV/YC); ≥90% forces the overall score into the red zone.

about.drilldown: The “Databases” table collects the same metrics per database as for the instance: cache hit ratio, dead tuples, vacuum staleness. Click a row and the selected database becomes the filter: the rules engine recomputes in its context, and the instance-only categories (Connections / Replication / Horizon / WAL / Locks) are hidden.
about.trendIntro: When a metrics datasource is configured, a trend appears under the gauge: score + seasonal baseline + latency. The seasonal baseline is the expected value of the metric at a specific point in the weekly cycle (load is cyclical: weekdays ≠ weekends, day ≠ night), not an overall average. It is built like this:
about.trendBucketing: Bucketing by “hour of week”: every history point lands in one of 168 buckets (7 days × 24 hours), hour_of_week = weekday×24 + hour.
about.trendMedian: The median per bucket over a long window (28 days by default) — robust to outliers: a one-off nightly VACUUM or a deploy does not move the baseline.
about.trendUsage: The current value is compared against its own baseline for that hour of the week, not against a global average:
about.trendDips: Dips: the score fell below the baseline for that hour of the week by more than the threshold → a red dot on the trend. A regular nightly batch is not a dip — its baseline is low too, so there is no false alarm.
about.trendLatency: Latency regression → performance: current latency / seasonal baseline. It works on any workload because it compares a query against itself rather than against an absolute 50/200/1000 ms threshold.
about.trendExample: For example: 50 ms on Monday at 14:00 (baseline 45 ms) is near normal; the same 50 ms on Monday at 03:00 (baseline 12 ms) is ~4× the baseline — a real anomaly. One value, two verdicts.
about.trendDegrade: The seasonal baseline and dips appear as history accumulates; while there is little of it the trend degrades gracefully — without the baseline line or dip markers.

databases.title: Databases
databases.worstHint: Worst database: {db} (score {score}). Click the row for details.

weights.tooltip: Category weights are normalised to 100%. Useful for, say, a replica where replication matters more than performance.
weights.readonlyHint: Only an administrator can change the weights.

inline.title: Breakdown
inline.pickDatabase: Pick a database in the selector above — this is a per-database query.
inline.empty: The query returned nothing — the problem does not reproduce right now.

high_connection_ratio.title: Connection pool is heavily loaded
high_connection_ratio.detail: Check whether the application is leaking connections (the pool is not closing them in time). If the growth is sustained, raise max_connections or put pgbouncer in front.

idle_in_transaction.title: Lingering idle in transaction
idle_in_transaction.detail: Transactions the application forgot about hold locks and block autovacuum. Check the application logs and whether the transactions are idempotent.

long_running_transaction.title: Long-running open transaction
long_running_transaction.short: The oldest transaction has been running for {metric_value} s.
long_running_transaction.detail: Find it via pg_stat_activity and decide whether it should be cancelled (pg_terminate_backend).

low_cache_hit_ratio.title: Low cache hit ratio
low_cache_hit_ratio.detail: Increase shared_buffers (usually 25% of RAM). Also look for tables with a high number of disk reads.

high_max_dead_ratio.title: High dead tuple ratio in a table
high_max_dead_ratio.detail: Run VACUUM ANALYZE on the affected table. Find it via pg_stat_user_tables.

high_avg_dead_ratio.title: High average dead ratio across the database
high_avg_dead_ratio.detail: Autovacuum is not keeping up. Revisit autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor and autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit.

many_bloated_tables.title: Many bloated tables
many_bloated_tables.detail: Go to Tables → Bloat and run VACUUM over the list.

replication_lag_time.title: High replication lag (time)
replication_lag_time.detail: Check the load on the replica (heavy SELECTs), the network, and wal_receiver_timeout.

replication_lag_bytes.title: High replication lag (bytes)
replication_lag_bytes.detail: If the lag keeps growing, increase max_wal_size or speed up WAL replay on the replica.

disconnected_replicas.title: Disconnected replicas
disconnected_replicas.short: Replicas not in the streaming state: {metric_value}.
disconnected_replicas.detail: Check network access from the replica to the primary, and the walreceiver status.

xid_wraparound_risk.title: XID wraparound risk
xid_wraparound_risk.detail: The thresholds mirror PostgreSQL's own mechanics: 150M — aggressive freezing begins (vacuum_freeze_table_age); 200M — emergency autovacuum kicks in (autovacuum_freeze_max_age); 1.6B — failsafe mode engages and index cleanup is skipped (vacuum_failsafe_age). At 2B the database goes read-only. Run VACUUM FREEZE on the affected databases.
xid_wraparound_risk.sql:
-- check XID age per database
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY xid_age DESC;

autovacuum_disabled.title: Autovacuum is disabled
autovacuum_disabled.short: autovacuum=off. Without it, dead tuples pile up until someone intervenes by hand.
autovacuum_disabled.detail: Enable autovacuum in postgresql.conf or via ALTER SYSTEM. Otherwise the database is condemned to manual VACUUM, and tables and indexes will bloat.
autovacuum_disabled.sql:
ALTER SYSTEM SET autovacuum = on;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

track_counts_disabled.title: track_counts is disabled
track_counts_disabled.short: Without usage statistics, autovacuum cannot decide which tables to clean.
track_counts_disabled.detail: track_counts is essential to autovacuum: it is how “active” databases and tables are identified. Turn it on.
track_counts_disabled.sql:
ALTER SYSTEM SET track_counts = on;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

tables_with_autovacuum_off.title: Tables with autovacuum disabled
tables_with_autovacuum_off.short: Found {metric_value} tables with autovacuum_enabled=false in their storage parameters.
tables_with_autovacuum_off.detail: This is rarely deliberate — it is usually a leftover from an old migration or experiment. Confirm that disabling it was justified.

relfrozenxid_age_outlier.title: Old table that has not been frozen
relfrozenxid_age_outlier.detail: Some tables have not been processed by autovacuum freeze for a long time. Find them and run VACUUM FREEZE.

horizon_lag_xids.title: The horizon is held back by a long transaction
horizon_lag_xids.short: The horizon is {metric_value} transactions behind — cleanup cannot remove dead tuples.
horizon_lag_xids.detail: Long transactions (Repeatable Read, or idle in transaction) hold back the database horizon, so cleanup skips dead tuples as “not yet removable”. Find the backend with the lowest backend_xmin and end it.
horizon_lag_xids.sql:
SELECT pid, usename, state, query, now() - xact_start AS duration
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_xmin IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY backend_xmin
LIMIT 5;

requested_checkpoint_ratio.title: Too many unscheduled checkpoints
requested_checkpoint_ratio.detail: Requested checkpoints happen when max_wal_size fills up. Increase max_wal_size or checkpoint_timeout. In a healthy system nearly every checkpoint is a timed one.

track_io_timing_disabled.title: track_io_timing is disabled
track_io_timing_disabled.short: Without it there are no detailed I/O timings in EXPLAIN ANALYZE or pg_stat_statements.
track_io_timing_disabled.detail: On modern operating systems the overhead is negligible. Turn it on to analyse I/O bottlenecks in detail.
track_io_timing_disabled.sql:
ALTER SYSTEM SET track_io_timing = on;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

vacuum_backlog.title: The vacuum backlog is growing
vacuum_backlog.detail: The number of tables that have crossed the autovacuum trigger threshold (on dead tuples or inserts, reloptions included). A deep backlog means autovacuum cannot keep up: raise autovacuum_max_workers, raise autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit, or lower autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay.

stale_vacuum.title: Vacuum has not run for a long time
stale_vacuum.detail: The age of the oldest table that has already crossed the autovacuum threshold but has not been vacuumed for a long time. If autovacuum cannot keep up, tighten autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor for the large tables.

tables_never_vacuumed.title: Tables never vacuumed
tables_never_vacuumed.short: Tables that have never been vacuumed: {metric_value}.
tables_never_vacuumed.detail: Run VACUUM ANALYZE by hand, or enable autovacuum if it is switched off at the table level.

active_lock_waiters.title: Active lock waits
active_lock_waiters.detail: A snapshot of pg_stat_activity. High values mean contention. Find the blocking processes and decide whether to end the long transactions.

longest_lock_wait_seconds.title: Long lock wait
longest_lock_wait_seconds.detail: One or more processes have been waiting for a lock far too long. Likely causes: a long transaction, heavy contention on a hot row, or a forgotten table lock (for example after CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY).

ungranted_locks.title: Ungranted locks in the queue
ungranted_locks.detail: The length of the wait queue. This differs from “active waiters” — a single process can wait on several locks.

deadlocks_rate.title: Deadlocks
deadlocks_rate.detail: Every deadlock points to an application problem (locks acquired in different orders). The details are in the server log. This is an absolute counter since the last pg_stat_database_reset; the reset time is on Home → Status / Stats reset.

lock_pool_saturation.title: The lock pool is near its limit
lock_pool_saturation.detail: The heavyweight lock pool is bounded by max_locks_per_transaction × max_connections. When it overflows, PostgreSQL raises “out of shared memory; consider increasing max_locks_per_transaction”. Raise max_locks_per_transaction (needs a restart) or reduce concurrency.

low_hot_update_ratio.title: Low share of HOT updates
low_hot_update_ratio.detail: A HOT update lets the new row version stay on the same page without writing to every index. A low share means indexes bloat and autovacuum spends more time. Lower the fillfactor for frequently updated tables that do not update indexed columns (70–90% is typical) — that reserves room for HOT updates.
low_hot_update_ratio.sql:
-- find tables with many non-HOT updates
SELECT schemaname, relname,
       n_tup_upd, n_tup_hot_upd,
       round(100.0 * n_tup_hot_upd::numeric / nullif(n_tup_upd, 0), 2) AS hot_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_tup_upd > 1000
ORDER BY hot_pct NULLS FIRST
LIMIT 20;

high_newpage_update_ratio.title: HOT chains break often
high_newpage_update_ratio.short: Share of updates moved to a new page: {metric_pct}%.
high_newpage_update_ratio.detail: PG 16+: the n_tup_newpage_upd counter shows how many UPDATEs did not fit on the original page — the HOT chain broke and an extra index entry was written. Lower the fillfactor of the affected table to reserve room on the page.

stale_planner_stats.title: Stale planner statistics
stale_planner_stats.detail: These tables are past their autoanalyze threshold (per-table settings honoured) and have not been analysed for over 3 hours — autoanalyze should have fired and did not. The planner may pick poor plans. Run ANALYZE by hand and find out why autoanalyze never reached the table (autovacuum_enabled=false, workers busy on large tables).

wal_level_minimal_with_replicas.title: wal_level=minimal while replicas exist
wal_level_minimal_with_replicas.detail: An incompatible configuration: wal_level=minimal does not support streaming replication. Replicas need replica or logical. Change the parameter (needs a restart).

wal_level_logical_without_publications.title: wal_level=logical without active publications
wal_level_logical_without_publications.short: wal_level=logical, but there are no active logical slots.
wal_level_logical_without_publications.detail: The logical WAL level costs overhead (more data in the journal, slower VACUUM). If logical replication is not in use, switch back to replica.

checksum_failures.title: Page checksum failures
checksum_failures.short: PostgreSQL detected data corruption (checksum failures). This is critical — check the disk and your backups.

host_cpu_saturation.title: Host CPU saturation
host_cpu_saturation.short: The 15-minute load average exceeds the core count (load/vCPU) — processes queue for CPU and latency grows.

pooler_saturation.title: Connection pool saturation
pooler_saturation.short: The pooler's server connections are close to the pool capacity — clients are queuing.

sequence_exhaustion.title: Sequence exhaustion (ID overflow)
sequence_exhaustion.short: A sequence is approaching the limit of its type (an int4 primary key, for example). An overflow stops writes — plan a migration to bigint.

latency_regression.title: Query latency regression
latency_regression.short: Query latency is above its seasonal baseline — check the top queries and the load.

seq_scan_regression.title: Sequential scans on large tables are growing
seq_scan_regression.short: The volume of rows read by sequential scans is above its seasonal baseline — indexes stopped being used, or the statistics went stale.
seq_scan_regression.detail: The seq-scan rate (tuples/s) is compared against its seasonal baseline (hour × weekday), so routine analytical scans are not treated as a regression. Growth usually means the planner abandoned an index (stale statistics → ANALYZE) or an index is missing or invalid. What to check: run ANALYZE on the hot tables; look at “Index usage” and the plans of the top queries (Seq Scan on large tables); add or rebuild an index if needed.

host_disk_space.title: Low free disk space on the host
host_disk_space.short: Disk utilisation: {metric_pct}% (LOW ≥70%, MED ≥80%, HIGH ≥90%). At ≥90% the overall Health Score is forced into the red zone.

trend.title: Health Score trend
trend.baselineHelp: The seasonal baseline is the median score per “hour × weekday” bucket over a long history window (so it follows the daily and weekly shape of the load). The current score is compared against the baseline for that same hour of the week; a drop below it by more than the threshold is flagged with a red dot (a dip).
trend.unavailable: The trend is unavailable: no metrics datasource is configured, or the target was not matched.

prefs.title: Settings
prefs.languageHint: If unset, the browser's language is used.
prefs.pageSizeHint: Affects how much is requested from the server, not just what is displayed.

tokens.title: Personal tokens of every user
tokens.revokeConfirm: Revoke the token “{name}” owned by {owner}? This cannot be undone.

users.title: Users
users.subject: User
users.name: Name
users.hint: The list grows as people sign in through SSO. Roles come from the identity provider and are not editable here.
